synopsis: Tracing the global aftermath of sustained contact between the Americas and the rest of the world, the book examines how crops, animals, diseases, people, and silver move across oceans after Columbus. It connects these exchanges to European expansion, upheaval in China and Africa, coerced labor, ecological transformation, and the emergence of an interdependent modern economy.

sources.title: 1493 by Charles C. Mann: 9780307278241 | PenguinRandomHouse.com: Books
1493: uncovering the new world Columbus created | Carnegie-Stout Public Library

synopsis: The book reconstructs the speculative boom, Wall Street crash, and political struggle that helped transform an apparent age of prosperity into the Great Depression. Following bankers, investors, officials, fraudsters, and skeptics, it examines how easy credit, ambition, ignored warnings, and battles between finance and government shaped the collapse and its national consequences.

sources.title: 1929 by Andrew Ross Sorkin: 9780593296967 | PenguinRandomHouse.com: Books

synopsis: In Oceania, records clerk Winston Smith rewrites history for the ruling Party while secretly questioning a society built on surveillance, propaganda, and control of thought. His private rebellion and forbidden relationship with Julia draw him into a dangerous struggle over memory, truth, and whether individual freedom can survive an all-encompassing state.

Organized as twenty-one essays, the book examines political, technological, social, and existential challenges shaping the present, including automation, data surveillance, nationalism, migration, terrorism, and ecological risk. It considers how people and institutions can preserve agency, meaning, sound judgment, and cooperation amid rapid change, information overload, and uncertainty about the future.

sources.title: 21 Lessons for the 21st Century by Yuval Noah Harari: 9780525512196 | PenguinRandomHouse.com: Books
21 Lessons for the 21st Century - Aurora Public Library - OverDrive

synopsis: The book presents a strategy framework for identifying the conditions that allow a company to earn durable returns despite competition. It explains seven forms of business power and considers when each can be established across a company’s development, linking invention, market timing, competitive barriers, and strategic choices to enduring value.

sources.title: 7 Powers by Hamilton Helmer | Open Library

synopsis: The memoir follows aeronautical engineer Joe Sutter from his early life into a Boeing career culminating in leadership of the team that develops the 747. It traces the aircraft through design, manufacturing, testing, certification, and entry into service while detailing the technical crises, corporate conflicts, production pressures, and broader changes reshaping commercial aviation.

sources.title: 747: creating the world's first jumbo jet and other adventures from a life in aviation | WorldCat.org
747: creating the world's first jumbo jet and other adventures from a life in aviation | Smithsonian Institution

synopsis: The guide frames the roughly 80,000 hours in a working life as an opportunity to build a satisfying career while addressing important problems. It challenges passion-first advice and offers frameworks for comparing problems and roles, testing personal fit, building flexible career capital, and choosing among direct work, advocacy, research, and earning to give.

sources.title: Announcing the new 80,000 Hours career guide
80,000 Hours – Find a fulfilling career that does good

synopsis: The book surveys research on average psychological and behavioral differences between men and women, covering mating, parenting, aggression, toy preferences, work, mental health, and equality. It evaluates evolutionary and social explanations for these patterns and considers how acknowledging population-level differences can coexist with removing bias, barriers, and restrictive gender expectations.

sources.title: A Billion Years of Sex Differences | Official Publisher Page | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: The book traces human intelligence through five evolutionary breakthroughs, beginning with early nervous systems and moving through reinforcement learning, simulation, social cognition, and language. It compares each biological development with capabilities and gaps in artificial intelligence, using the brain’s evolutionary history to explain why machines excel at some tasks yet still struggle with flexible, humanlike understanding.

sources.title: A Brief History of Intelligence: Evolution, AI, and The Five Breakthroughs That Made Our Brains — Max Bennett
A Brief History of Intelligence by Max Solomon Bennett | Goodreads
